channel.title: Latest Updates
channel.description: The most recent blogs and online articles from The Economist

item.title: Plot Twist newsletter: The art of adolescence
item.description: Caitlin Talbot, our digital culture correspondent, visits the worldâs first museum of youth culture

item.title: Is fortified infant formula worth it?
item.description: Perhaps less than some marketers claim

item.title: J.D. Vanceâs second coming
item.description: âCommunionâ, his latest book, shows how the vice-president fails to notice his own vices

item.title: Six films you will actually enjoy watching with your children
item.description: Our list features magical powers, musicals and mysteries

item.title: The battle for blue America
item.description: Our weekly podcast on America. This week, primary challenges and the insurgent left

item.title: What to watch this week
item.description: These films and TV shows explore ambition

item.title: The real villain in âToy Story 5â is not whom you would expect
item.description: Why Pixarâs latest romp is one of the best animated films ever

item.title: The deceptive rise of the âshort kingâ
item.description: Small men have won over social media, but continue to struggle in the dating market

item.title: Coming in Andy: Britainâs prime minister-in-waiting
item.description: Also on the daily podcast: the Iran dealâs effect on a battered economy and a tour of Barack Obamaâs presidential library

item.description: Our daily political update, featuring the stories that matter

item.title: The strange disappearance of Japanâs animators
item.description: Anime has never been more popular. So why does the industry have a labour crisis?

item.title: Andy Burnham is now Britainâs prime-minister-in-waiting
item.description: After his by-election win he is set to challenge Sir Keir Starmer for the party (and the countryâs) leadership

item.title: The G7 has nudged open a window for diplomacy in Ukraine
item.description: Europeans see a hopeful shift in Americaâs attitude

item.title: How quickly could you burn through $2.4trn?
item.description: Our podcast on markets, the economy and business. This week, the clock is ticking for Americaâs social security fund

item.title: Economic data, commodities and markets
Harlan Coben, Netflixâs new darling, has a mystery you can solve
item.description: Why are sometimes cheesy thrillers set in Britain so utterly gripping?

item.title: Adults are going back to summer camp
item.description: Retreats designed for grown-ups are growing in popularity

item.title: What the largest-ever shareholder judgment reveals about Russia
item.description: If you want to see Vladimir Putinâs soul, study the fate of Yukos, an oil company

item.title: Barack Obama has built a monument to himself
item.description: He is just the latest president to do so

item.title: How plants keep tabs on the competition
item.description: They grow faster when their rivals are doing the same

item.title: How to launch a tech product
item.description: Create a slick, irony-free corporate safe space

item.title: The Iran war has boosted Equinor, Norwayâs energy giantÂ
item.description: But it cannot turn a greying cash cow into a sprightly upstart

item.title: Meet the worldâs new peacemakers
item.description: Autocracies are taking over conflict mediation. The deals they are making look different

item.title: News extortion is rife in China
item.description: The countryâs censors may be making it worse

item.title: How China still outworks the West
item.description: A medal scheme masks the identity of its toughest labourers

item.title: From Philippine staple to global sensation: the rise of ube
item.description: The country is struggling to keep upÂ with soaring demand

item.title: Taiwanâs opposition leader faced a tough crowd in America
item.description: Many still have doubts about her outreach to Xi Jinping

item.title: Myanmarâs junta revives a hated dam to court China
item.description: A dam halted by protests in 2011 is back, but not for the energy it will produce

item.title: The real winner in Myanmarâs civil war is China
item.description: It doesnât want to fix the war-torn country but is fine managing it

item.title: The Colombian diaspora is overwhelmingly right-wing
item.description: But not in Russia

item.title: The Gen-Z streamer from MedellÃ­n influencing Colombiaâs election
item.description: Algorithms, not debates, will determine the countryâs next president

item.title: Is the staple meal in Nigeria and Ghana becoming a luxury?
item.description: What the rising prices of jollof rice show about Nigeria and Ghanaâs economies

item.title: The Iran war meant an economic crisis for Africa
item.description: It was the third shock of the 2020s to hit the continentÂ

item.title: A flimsy deal will stop the bombing and restart the oil
item.description: Whether it can do anything more will be the question that defines the next few months

item.title: The left is coming for Democratic incumbents
item.description: In primary challenges, Mayor Zohran Mamdani is trying to install allies in Congress

item.title: Pet-custody laws in America are changing
item.description: More states are weighing petsâ interests, not just their price

item.title: Populist president, meet socialist mayor
item.description: Americaâs capital is poised to elect a new leftist leader

item.title: Europeans should learn to love the air-conditioner
item.description: Green electricity means never having to say sorry for lowering the thermostat

item.title: Britainâs departure made Europe more French
item.description: A smaller EU has learned to live without Britain

item.title: Britain is slipping down the defence league table
item.description: The government cannot avoid hard choices for ever

item.title: Anthropicâs astonishing commercial success makes it a target
item.description: The cultlike firm is battling with the government for control of superpowered AI

item.title: Were we too quick to attack âGen Z socialismâ?
item.description: Also this week Trump and judges, crime in Britain, âStar Trekâ and war, Ethiopiaâs prime minister, edible medical devices

item.title: Donât restrict Chinese biotech
item.description: Patients benefit from faster, cheaper treatments, wherever they are invented

item.title: Britain is not ready to rejoin the EU
item.description: After a lost decade, it is time to focus on the future

item.title: AI has granted America vast new power
item.description: Its government is now the gatekeeper to frontier modelsâand most compute

item.description: In many small ways, and mostly for the worse

item.description: How Tocquevilleâs âtyranny of the majorityâ shows up at Harvard today

item.description: A rural township fights over a very modern frontier

item.description: After four years of institutional decay, fiscal recklessness and deteriorating security, the country must change course, argues IvÃ¡n Duque

item.description: Our daily political update, featuring the stories that matter

item.title: War has strengthened the Islamic Republic. Peace could split it
item.description: For now, the hardliners are in the ascendant

item.title: A new golden age for Japanese banks comes with a catch
item.description: Smaller lenders are saddled with low-return bonds they cannot sell

item.title: Europe buys the future, America builds it
item.description: Does that matter? A high-stakes transatlantic spat has erupted over GDP figures

item.title: Tataâs big bets are yet to pay off
item.description: The Indian conglomerateâs boardroom drama highlights a speculative strategy

item.description: The conflict will have stubborn political after-effects

item.description: Family-run conglomerates make the stock market a tricky place to invest

item.description: They want to turn back the clock on Brexit. Winning campaigns deal in the futureÂ

item.description: For a start, the government needs to think bigger

item.description: The populists promise a âscorching hot socialist summerâ of protest

item.title: Iranâs battered economy will take years to recover from the war
item.description: Its leaders want sanctions relief and aid for reconstruction

item.title: Ancient DNA is rewriting the history of plague
item.description: Dense cities do not seem to have been necessary for outbreaks of the disease

item.title: The chocolate industry is built on the labour of bloodsucking midges
item.description: Researchers have now confirmed how cacao plants are pollinated

item.title: Fox, Roku and the next phase of the streaming wars
item.description: A $22bn deal creates a formidable new power in Hollywood

item.title: Why Westerners are falling for love scams
item.description: Romance fraud is driven by poverty in west Africa and loneliness in the West, a new book argues

item.description: Our podcast on science and technology. This week, why AI superintelligence might be much closer than people realise

item.description: Every day we will deliver concise analysis of the stories driving business and markets worldwideÂ
